text: Hi, my name is E. David Crawford, and during the next few minutes, I would like to review with you a technique that we have developed called the anatomic radical prostatectomy. We do this procedure through a six to seven centimeter lower abdominal incision. We use a hand port for exposure and specially developed laparoscopic instruments. A key part of this operation is an instrument known as the thunder beat. And during the procedure, I'll show you how we use this thunder beat to facilitate the operation. This is the ThunderBeat. In one handpiece, it combines both bipolar radio frequency and ultrasonic technologies. It provides for very rapid and effective sealing and cutting of tissue and vessels. As a matter of fact, it can seal vessels up to seven millimeters. There are two buttons on the ThunderBeat. The first one is the purple button, which combines both technologies, so we get effective coagulation and cutting. And then if you want coagulation alone, the bipolar radio frequency is activated by the blue button. Sometimes we use this when we're deep in the pelvis and we advance and do the coagulation, back off a little bit and do the cutting. But the instrument actually is very effective using the combined technologies with the purple button. During the video, we will see how we have adapted the procedure and utilized this Thunderbeak for effective tissue sealing. We've looked at spread of energy and heat. We'll describe that, and we'll also talk about how it speeds up the time of the surgery.

The surgeon measures and makes a seven-centimeter incision above the pubic bone, accessing the retropubic space. A hand port is inserted for exposure, minimizing trauma compared to other retractors. The bladder neck is visualized, with the index finger on the catheter balloon, and the prostate visible at the top left of the screen. A BOVI device is used to incise and isolate the bladder neck, while the ThunderBeat effectively coagulates tissue around it. The urethra is seen entering the bladder neck, and the ThunderBeat and Kittner are used to isolate it. The urethra is sharply divided with a knife, avoiding proximity to the prostate to prevent positive margins. The catheter is released and pulled forward for traction, aiding in the division of the remaining urethra and Bell's muscle. The bladder neck begins to fall away, and a new Foley catheter is inserted into the bladder neck, with care taken not to use it for traction.

The surgeon drains urine from the incision area and proceeds through the posterior bladder neck to isolate the right vas deferens and seminal vesicles. The right vas is grasped with a Babcock clamp, elevated, and dissected using the ThunderBeat device, which quickly seals the tissue. The stump of the vas is secured with another Babcock clamp. The seminal vesicle is dissected out, and the ThunderBeat is used to coagulate and cut the artery to the seminal vesicle. The surgeon approaches the lateral aspects of the prostate, being cautious of the left neurovascular bundle, and uses the ThunderBeat to free the prostate tissue. Thermocouples are placed to monitor temperature changes during the procedure.

The surgeon uses the ThunderBeat device to dissect the right lateral aspects of the prostate, carefully pushing away the neurovascular bundle. The seminal vesicles are being dissected, and the catheter is visible in place. A number two Prolene suture is placed in the base of the prostate for traction. The surgeon gently manipulates the neurovascular structures with a Kittner, working through the endopelvic fascia towards the puboprostatic and posterior regions of the prostate. Small vessels feeding posteriorly are coagulated using the ThunderBeat.

The surgeon uses a sponge stick to push down on the rectum, exposing tissue for coagulation with the Thunder Beat. The left side of the prostate is dissected, pushing away neurovascular bundles and coming through the endopelvic fascia. The prostate is mostly free except for the apex and dorsal venous complex. A Babcock is placed at the twelve o'clock position on the prostate for traction, and the Thunder Beat is used to release the puboprostatic ligament. The dorsal venous complex is addressed with the Thunder Beat, and the urethra and apex are visually identified.

The surgeon uses the ThunderBeat to mop up residual tissue and bleeding around the urethra and apex. The urethra is visible, appearing plump and thick, which is beneficial for postoperative continence. Small vessels are divided, and the urethra at the apex is sharply incised. The catheter is visible, and the surgeon places dyed two-oh monocryl stitches at various clock positions around the urethra, pulling the catheter back partially. The ThunderBeat is used to divide remaining urethral tissue, minimizing bleeding, and the prostate is removed with proline stitches in place, showing good margins.

The surgeon everts the bladder mucosa using 3-0 Vicryl sutures, creating a mucosa-to-mucosa anastomosis to reduce the risk of bladder neck contractures. Four stitches are placed to achieve a nicely inverted mucosa. A free needle is used to complete the stitching, starting with the posterior stitches at five-thirty and six-thirty, followed by the eight o'clock stitch. A catheter is inserted, and the operation is completed with a drain left in place temporarily.

So in summary, the anatomic radical prostatectomy does offer an alternative to other ways of removing the prostate including purely laparoscopic and robotic prostatectomy. Our results are good. The blood loss, the discharge dates and so forth are very similar to laparoscopic and robotic with most patients going home. within uh... twenty four hours the next day uh... getting back to activities in two to three weeks uh... our experience with uh... continents and potency appears to be very similar with reported with other uh... some of these other techniques uh... so this uh... technique uh... uh... is really uh... something that is uh... It's new and exciting and what really makes it possible are the new surgical instruments that we have, most notably recently the ThunderBeat. The ThunderBeat allows for very safe and complete coagulation and cutting. It's used not only for this anatomic prostatectomy but also use it in radical cystectomies, and in radical nephrectomies and other oncologic surgeries. So it does offer a very nice advantage when doing these procedures.
